reasoning:
Reproduced the scenario frame by frame; every step holds.

(1) The diff is real: commit b097efb ("tts: fix double-speak") changed handleSpeak in src/App.tsx (now lines 239-251) from always calling speakText(text, ttsRate, ttsVolume, ttsLanguage||'english') plus ws.speak, to an either/or: when ws.isConnected, ONLY ws.speak(text) fires; speakText (browser speechSynthesis) runs only when disconnected. Verified via `git show b097efb` and the current file.

(2) speakText (App.tsx:143-159) was the only language-aware path: it sets u.lang='hi-IN' for ttsLanguage='hindi' and auto-detects Devanagari (/[à¤€-à¥¿]/) for 'auto'. Grep confirms the only call site of speakText/speechSynthesis in src/ is handleSpeak's else branch â€” no screen speaks directly. All screens' onSpeak props thread back to handleSpeak (App.tsx renderScreen common props, PersistentEmergencyButton at App.tsx:423, GlobalNavBar via each screen).

(3) The backend path carries no language: ws.speak = (text) => send('speak', { text }) (src/hooks/useWebSocket.tsx:608). python/main.py:1860 routes to _speak (main.py:2560-2566) -> tts.speak -> worker loop engine.say/runAndWait (main.py:713-717) with one voice pinned at _init_engine (main.py:686-705, 'female' name preference). Grep for hindi|hi-IN|lang in python/main.py: zero matches â€” no language switching exists anywhere in the backend.

(4) Emergency phrase: GlobalNavBar.tsx:195-201 builds `${enPhrase} ... ${hiPhrase}` (Devanagari) when settings.showHindi, then onSpeak(fullPhrase) -> handleSpeak. In the normal full-app state the Python WebSocket backend is connected (it IS the gaze pipeline), so this goes exclusively to pyttsx3/SAPI5.

(5) Empirically re-verified the silence on this machine (independent of the first reviewer): installed SAPI desktop voices are only Microsoft David/Zira (en-US). System.Speech (same SAPI engine pyttsx3 drives) synthesizing the Hindi emergency phrase produced a 46-byte WAV (header only, zero audio frames); the English phrase produced 187,420 bytes; the mixed EN+HI emergency phrase produced exactly 187,420 bytes â€” byte-identical to English-only, proving the Hindi half contributes zero audio.

Net effect: with backend connected (the normal state), the Hindi half of the emergency call-for-help is silent, and any pure-Hindi utterance (Hindi phrases, Hindi keyboard text, ttsLanguage='hindi'/'auto') is total silence. The ttsLanguage setting is dead code in connected mode, and no installable Hindi voice pack can fix it because the protocol/backend never selects a voice by language. Before the commit, the browser path tagged hi-IN and would use a Hindi voice whenever installed; after, Hindi is unspeakable in connected mode regardless of installed voices. This breaks the documented bilingual requirement and the emergency path. (Caveat noted for fairness: on this exact dev rig, which has no Hindi voice at all, pure-Hindi was likely silent via the browser path too â€” but the mixed emergency phrase regression manifests on any rig, and the structural regression makes Hindi unfixable-by-voice-install on the patient's rig.) The commit did fix a real double-speak bug, but the fix removed the only Hindi-capable path instead of carrying language over the protocol.
suggestedFix: Carry language (and rate/volume) over the speak protocol and make the backend language-aware, with a browser fallback for languages the backend voice cannot render. Concretely: (a) src/hooks/useWebSocket.tsx:608 â€” change to send('speak', { text, language, rate, volume }) and pass ttsLanguage/ttsRate/ttsVolume from App.tsx handleSpeak; (b) python/main.py TTSEngine â€” on each speak command, if language=='hindi' or the text contains Devanagari (re [à¤€-à¥¿]), enumerate voices and switch to a hi-IN voice for that utterance, restoring the default afterward; if no hi-IN voice is installed, return a 'tts_unsupported_language' reply so the frontend can fall back; (c) minimal immediate mitigation in src/App.tsx handleSpeak â€” when connected, route any text containing Devanagari through speakText (browser, u.lang='hi-IN') instead of ws.speak (for the mixed emergency phrase, split on the ' ... ' separator: English half to ws.speak, Hindi half to speakText) so Hindi audio is never dropped while still avoiding double-speak for English. Add a regression test/checklist item: with backend connected and showHindi on, the emergency button must produce audible Hindi.
